The Problem

Sound familiar?

Most real estate teams don't fall apart because of bad agents or a slow market.

They fall apart because the person leading the team is also running the backend — and nobody can do both at full capacity.

"That's not a discipline problem. That's an operational gap. And it's exactly what I fix."
Pain Point 01

You're the default for everything — agent questions, client follow ups, transaction deadlines, inbox fires. All of it lands on you.

Pain Point 02

Leads are going cold not because your agents aren't good — but because nobody owns the follow up when it gets busy.

Pain Point 03

You know the backend needs to be tighter — but every time you try to fix it something more urgent comes up.

Services

Here's what changes when I'm running your backend.

Every service is built around ownership — not task completion. I don't wait to be told. I take responsibility for the outcome and make sure it gets done.

Service 01

No Lead Goes Cold On My Watch

I own your CRM completely — daily updates, follow up tasks, lead status changes, database organization. Every lead in your pipeline gets touched. Nothing sits untouched. Nothing slips through.

"Because the difference between a cold lead and a closed deal is usually just one follow up that never happened."

Service 02

Deals Don't Fall Through Because of Me

I track every active transaction from contract to close — deadlines, missing documents, agent coordination, vendor follow ups. I own the timeline so you never have to chase it.

"Because one missed deadline doesn't just cost a deal — it costs the referral that deal would have generated."

Service 03

You Stop Being The Default For Everything

I monitor your inbox and calendar daily — flagging what needs you, handling what doesn't, and making sure nothing urgent disappears into an unread pile. Agent questions, vendor requests, scheduling — I'm the first filter.

"Because your time is worth too much to spend on things that don't require you."

Overview

The Hartwell Group is a 6-agent real estate team known for their high volume transaction work and strong community presence. Seeking to scale their operation without burning out their team lead, they needed dedicated backend support to handle the operational load quietly costing them deals, referrals, and leadership focus.

This case study highlights my expertise in real estate backend operations, demonstrating how I helped The Hartwell Group eliminate critical operational gaps, recover significant leadership time, and build systems that keep the team running at full capacity.

Objectives
  • Identify and eliminate the operational gaps costing the team lead time, focus, and closed deals.
  • Rebuild the CRM database so every lead has an owner, a status, and a clear next step — nothing sitting untouched.
  • Create a transaction coordination system that removes manual tracking entirely and eliminates missed deadlines.
  • Establish an inbox and communication triage protocol so the team lead stops being the default contact for every agent question and vendor request.
  • Recover leadership time and redirect it toward revenue generating activities — showings, negotiations, and relationship building.
Strategy and Approach

To address The Hartwell Group's operational challenges, we developed a focused strategy built around four key areas:

  • Conducting a full audit of the existing CRM — Follow Up Boss — to map every lead, identify dormant contacts, and rebuild the pipeline into a clean and workable system.
  • Mapping all active transactions to identify gaps in deadline tracking, missing documents, and coordination breakdowns between agents, vendors, and clients.
  • Reviewing the team lead's inbox and communication patterns to understand volume, recurring question types, and time spent on tasks that didn't require leadership involvement.
  • Building a week by week implementation plan prioritizing the highest impact areas first — CRM revival, then transaction coordination, then inbox management — so results were visible from day one.
Implementation
Week 1 — CRM Audit and Lead Revival

A full audit of Follow Up Boss identified 63 leads with zero activity in over 30 days and no follow up tasks assigned. Every dormant lead was reviewed, tagged by status and priority, and assigned a follow up task with a clear next step. A contact segmentation system was built to keep the pipeline organized and workable on an ongoing basis.

Week 2 — Transaction Coordination System

A transaction tracking dashboard was built in Google Sheets mapping all 8 active deals with key dates, outstanding documents, and next action items. A standard transaction checklist was created covering every step from contract to close. Weekly deadline reminders were established for every active transaction so nothing moved forward without full visibility.

Week 3 — Inbox and Communication Management

Inbox folders and priority labels were set up to triage incoming messages by urgency. Standard response templates were created for the 10 most common agent questions — eliminating the need for the team lead to personally handle routine communications. A daily inbox review protocol was established with urgent items flagged and actioned within 2 hours.

Results
  • The CRM was fully rebuilt with 63 dormant leads re-engaged — two were reactivated and moved back into the active pipeline within the first week.
  • Transaction deadline tracking improved from a manual spreadsheet with two missed deadlines to zero missed deadlines across all 8 active deals.
  • Team lead daily admin time reduced from 3 to 4 hours to under 1 hour — recovering approximately 15 hours of focused sales time per week.
  • Inbox backlog of 80+ unread emails was cleared within 24 hours and maintained at zero daily.
  • The transaction coordination system and CRM management process were permanently adopted by the team.

Dormant leads63 untouchedAll re-engaged

Lessons Learned
  • The biggest operational gap in most real estate teams is not a lack of tools — it is a lack of ownership. CRMs go unused and leads go cold not because the systems don't work but because nobody is consistently assigned to run them.
  • Taking ownership of backend tasks compounds quickly. Within three weeks the team lead had recovered more than 15 hours per week simply by having one person responsible for areas that had previously fallen to whoever had time.
  • A thorough audit before taking action is critical. The first week revealed gaps that were not visible on the surface — dormant leads that looked active, transactions missing key documents, and inbox patterns consuming far more leadership time than anyone had tracked.
Conclusion

By taking full ownership of The Hartwell Group's backend operations, we eliminated the process gaps quietly costing the team deals and leadership focus. Through a systematic CRM revival, a purpose built transaction coordination system, and a daily inbox management protocol, the team lead recovered significant time, the pipeline became workable and visible, and no deal moved forward without full coordination.

This is the operational gap I work with. If any of this sounds familiar — the pilot week is the fastest way to see what's possible for your operation. Start with one week. $300. No long term commitment.
